item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Needs quite a bit of correction

"The language spoken in the video are is currently unidentified, but appear to be a composite of spoken Greek, Latin, and several unknown languages."
Is this canonly just one language spoken in the video or multiple languages?

"The following is a amended testing log" AN amended, my friend. It is "an" instead of "a" when the first letter of the next word is a vowel.

"on 14/06/2000 at at 39.7████ N. 11.5████ E" at at rattatat tat

Anyway, I'd fix these but i do need to wait for confirmation as to whether or not language in the first thing us meant to be plural or singular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The point doesn't come across at all and it has some mistakes. Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can't blame an article for following common practice of the time it was written. The great crosslinking thing is recent; before that, there was no canon, crosslinks were harshly frowned upon, and this would have been downvoted for containing them.

Not meaning to be a dick to you, but this comment really makes me worry that old articles will be judged unfairly by these new standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like the concept, but I think it would have been better tying into tech left over by SCP-1000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do not understand the video footage from bone building blocks. o_0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it confusing, and couldn't figure out the intention. Then I read your spoiler and didn't like it much. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with Silent Mic. I would add that by authoring a 'building block' SCP you've made it quite complicated, and yet requires more detail. I was fuzzy on how the flesh and bone knitted to form these objects/uses. On a purely amusing note, I found myself imagining the built fountains going awry and forming the Mediterranean Sea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the real hurdle is making this even feasible. I've re-read the SCP twice, read the discussions, and I still cannot picture this at all. I get the idea, but say it out loud: "An ancient civilization that built VCRs and cars from bone that mutated to fleshy constructs." It's not a simple aspect of humanity that can be relatable, it's just weird idea piled on weird idea piled on weird idea. You clearly care about doing this right, but I dunno if it can be tackled. Maybe change something about the process, or the source. "Constructs could only be seeded to develop if the cornerstone bone (cornerbone!) came from a tribal member, given voluntarily, and blessed by the local medicine man". Then you can do the whole "they were their own downfall, destroying themselves to build their glory" angle. Little bit more of a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've updated this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A minor change that might do a lot to better telegraph your intent:

The "Turret" launches -098 at a target, but -098 is not one of the things you explain. If you more clearly establish that -098 is food, or at least something less-than-weapons-grade, it could provide a big clue that it isn't the weapons platform The Foundation has taken it for.

Maybe make it have a connection for the "fountain", then it would be a beer cannon and they could mistake it for a riot pacification weapon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Craigslist.

Oh, you mean in the context of the article ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where did you get "giant flesh anus" from, exactly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, that was NOT a twist I was expecting. I'm not sure who would want to party by guzzling booze out of a giant flesh anus or want food shot at them from another giant flesh anus, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could do advertisements of some sort or something. I'll think on it.

I'm not sure if the time context is correct, but I was thinking more Roman than Greek, so Bacchus rather than Dionysus would fit better in this context if I decide to go down that route.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm, that might work. An isolated sub-culture, sort of a cross between Atlantis and Lake Havasu during spring break, but without the culturally built-in time limit. I picture a place where all the frat-y sons of noblemen and rich merchants go after they steal the key to Dad's treasure house or get shipped off to so that they will stop embarrasing the family with endless drunken buffonery.

But the real difficulty with that is how do you point the reader at it without just coming out and saying it?

Edit: I now imagine it was a special place set aside because it was "sacred to Dionysus". Is that the right god for the era, or would it be the other side of the Greco-Roman deity equivalency table?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I think I've been explaining this wrong. I don't mean civilization as in a whole big empire that is just these guys, I mean that the people who use these things are the ones being referred to here. They were just a smallish group located off the coast of Italy. I know that, of course, an entire culture can't actually be based solely on frat boys and partiers. Eh, anyways, I like the idea less and less the more I talk about it, and might focus on the fact that there were people who were intelligent and innovative enough to be able to create organic machines. Or something. I dunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I'm pretty sure I hate that concept.

In the first place, I'm pretty meh on the "misinterpreting artifacts" plot device. It's not quite "done to death" category, but it is certainly a "been done".
Second, that is a nonsensical culture. Frat boys only work as a sub-culture within a larger, less douchey, society. A civilization that hyper-focuses on one niche group is certainly plausible, even historically accurate, but to be believable it has to be a niche with mechanisms to fulfill the basic functions. A warrior centered civilzation can get away with having few or no farmers, carpenters, bakers, etc. because they can kill their neighbors and take their food and goods. A scholarly society can trade knowledge for material needs. A priestly folk can either rely on charitable donations or threaten with being selected "by the Gods" for the next sacrifice, depending on the religion. But party-dudes are jerks with nothing to offer or hold over the productive folks. Who makes the bones for them if all they care about is the rager?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I swear, I'm working on clarifying this. Here's the answer under a spoiler.
Are you disappointed with the article now

The idea is that the Foundation ran with a conclusion when testing this SCP object that was false. The conclusion was, of course, that the civilization that created SCP-1705 had a strong, serious warrior culture and mindset based on the objects created. However, they were dead wrong.

The people who made these things were just partiers, man. I mean, the things described are supposed to be (similar to) a neverending supply of alcohol, fireworks, a TV/VCR, and a meat/food cannon, respectively. These guys were probably trained to fight, yes, but that's not where the majority of their time went to. They were just set on having a blast with life.

The other things I plan to include that hopefully will make this more clear:

  • Bongs (either "incense" for the temple or "sedatives")
  • Vomit/urine receptacles
  • Basic sound system1

And now, to patiently wait and watch as the rating on this article plummets.

Footnotes
1. Very unsure on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last note doesn't make sense to me. What other possible interpretations are there? The function of all items seems very straight-forward and self-evident, and taken together they paint a very solid picture of a decadent and violent culture that self-destructed. If you have an alternate reading in mind, I for one need more hints.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose that's an interpretation. Anyways, thanks to eveyone commentint on this. I'm working on adding some more meat to the article in my sandbox, and will update this page when I add in those changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've told a summary of the story of this civilization with the item log. Flesh it out a bit, pardon the pun. Give us some more items. Yes, the fountain helped them be more complacent. Yes, they had this ruling class that was very proud. Give us some more. I feel like this could be really interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I brought this up in chat. The quote by the archeologist doesn't make any sense because "warrior civilization" is not a term used by archeologists. Warrior is a class or caste not a term used to describe a civilization. "Warrior Culture" makes more sense. I also don't know why warriors off the coast of Italy during Pax Romana is strange. The Romans had armies why can't some random civilization of bone-smiths have them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Give the site a week. You'll probably have an answer by then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems to be a common complaint. I'll work on adding in more depth soon enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels very bare-bones, if you'll excuse the phrase. You cover a lot of ground in the description, but it doesn't engage me or provide much reward. It feels like a morbid D&D magic item with a message that doesn't fully come across, rather than a story in the SCP format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All of my puns are on purpose. Why would we have an MTF that actually specialized in synchronized swimming?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Sinkronized"

Was this on purpose?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it enough to upvote it, but it does lack something. It's just too…neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "All instances SCP-1705" missing an "of"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think a weak hook is your problem… the object itself is a bit underwhelming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers ahoy!
I'm far too subtle for my own good.
  • It's a depressant. Like alcohol.
  • That specific instance. It can be built again. Like a party popper or a firecracker. You can always make more, given enough of the right kind of bones.
  • It's like a VCR. Except not. But imagine that, if it helps.
  • It didn't; individual things manifested along with the rest of the building. And for all sakes and purposes, they are basically fleshy VCRs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The liquid has been revealed to be highly addictive and toxic.

First, what sort of effect does it have? (That is, stimulant, depressant, etc) Second, that should probably read "toxic and highly addictive", since right now it can be read as it being highly toxic, and if it was highly toxic then subjects probably wouldn't stay alive long enough to become addicted.

Usage of an instance SCP-1705-B-071 renders that instance unusable.

Does that mean that the bones used to make it are destroyed, or that the bones can be recovered, but the instructions used to make that particular configuration won't work again?

Attached to SCP-1705-B-090 is an input device and a module for housing recorded media.

What sort of input device? Something that looks like an ear, and you speak into it? Something that looks like an eye?

Instances of SCP-1705-B-090 have been found in the majority of the homes with accompanying media.

First, I thought that the communications device didn't have any media available. Second, what do the media look like? Fleshy VCR tapes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still don't really care… novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept quite a bit, but this is missing something, or maybe it's an execution thing. It certainly reads rather choppily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that was weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is a good commentary on how many archaeologists back in the day extended their own interpretations to ancient artifacts without first considering other interpretations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've added in an addendum to make the hook more obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like SCP-140 but more boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no hook here. I don't have an ohwow moment. It's well written, but it doesn't make me care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one just doesn't grab me. It sounded a lot more interesting as a concept than it actually does in execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. Look near the end of the first description paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. Bone legos. Somewhat interesting, thought it doesn't really grab me. You say the structures are made of animalian tissue, but the building blocks are just bones. Does the tissue grow on afterward?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Done in response to Djoric's challenge of rewritten a deleted Pre-Mass edit article, http://scpclassic.wikidot.com/scp-605-s Actually, this thing has deviated so much from that one that I'm not even sure it counts.

Anyways, thanks to everyone that looked at this for me.
